subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 18! This is a special project unit. Instead of only reading and answering questions, you are going to become a real probability investigator. You will ask a question about chance, make a careful guess about what will happen, run your own experiment, collect your own data, and then work out what your results tell you. By the end you will have carried out a whole investigation from the very first question to the final report, just like a true scientist.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Plan a simple probability investigation by choosing a clear question.
Make a sensible prediction (a hypothesis) before you begin.
Decide on your materials and how many trials to carry out.
Collect data neatly using tally charts and frequency tables.
Work out the experimental probability of an outcome (how often it happened out of all your trials).
Compare your results with the theoretical probability (what should happen in a fair test).
Draw a fair conclusion that answers your question using your data as evidence.
Present your findings clearly using tables, simple graphs and words.
Spot how probability is used in the real world all around you.

concepts.title: The Investigator's Cycle
concepts.explanation: Every probability investigation, big or small, follows the same clear path. We call it the investigator's cycle because once you reach the end you can start again with a new question. Here are the six steps you will use again and again in this unit: Predict what you think will happen, and why.
concepts.example: Experiment – plan it carefully and carry it out.

concepts.title: Asking a Good Question and Making a Prediction
concepts.explanation: Every investigation begins with a question. But not every question can be tested with an experiment. A good probability question is clear, is about chance, and can be tested by doing something many times. Compare these two questions: “Why is the sky blue?” cannot be tested by flipping or spinning, so it is not a probability question. But “When I roll a die, does each number come up about the same number of times?” is perfect – it is clear, it is about chance, and you can test it by rolling a die again and again.…
concepts.example: Is this coin fair? Does it land heads about as often as tails?

concepts.title: Planning a Fair Experiment
concepts.explanation: Once you know your question and your prediction, you must plan the experiment. Good planning is what separates a trustworthy investigation from a messy one. Before you touch a coin or a die, decide these five things and write them in your journal: What you will do – for example, flip a coin and look at which side lands up.
concepts.example: What counts as an outcome – here, heads or tails.

concepts.title: Collecting and Organising Your Data
concepts.explanation: Now comes the exciting part – running the experiment and collecting data. Data is just a grown-up word for the results you gather. The secret to good data is to record it neatly and as you go, never from memory afterwards. The best tool for this is the tally chart. A tally chart lets you count quickly without losing your place. Each time an outcome happens, you make one small vertical mark next to it. When you reach five, you draw the fifth mark across the other four, making a neat gate of five (IIII). Counting in…
concepts.example: The last column shows the frequency of each outcome – that is, how many times it happened. Heads happened 11 times, tails happened 9 times, and 11 + 9 = 20, which matches the number of trials. Checking that your…

concepts.title: Working Out Experimental Probability
concepts.explanation: Once your data is collected and counted, you can work out the experimental probability of each outcome. This is the chance of an outcome based on what actually happened in your experiment. The rule is simple and you will use it many times: Experimental probability = frequency ÷ total number of trials.
concepts.example: In words: take how many times an outcome happened, and divide it by how many trials you did altogether. Let us use Amina's coin data. She got 11 heads out of 20 flips, so the experimental probability of heads is 11 out…

concepts.title: Comparing With Theoretical Probability and Drawing Conclusions
concepts.explanation: So far you have found the experimental probability – what actually happened. There is a second kind of chance called the theoretical probability – what should happen if everything is perfectly fair. For a fair coin, the theoretical probability of heads is exactly one half, because there are two equal sides. For a fair die, each number has a theoretical probability of one out of six, because there are six equal faces. For a fair four-colour spinner, each colour has a theoretical probability of one out of four. The…
concepts.example: Look carefully. Green came up more often than expected (0.35 against 0.25) and yellow came up less often (0.15 against 0.25). Does this prove the spinner is unfair? Not quite. With only 40 spins, some difference from…

methods.title: How to run a probability investigation
methods.example: Idris says, “I wonder about my die.” Turn this into a clear, testable probability question.
steps: Ask a clear question about chance.
Make a prediction, with a reason.
Plan a fair experiment and choose plenty of trials.
Record every result with a tally chart.
Work out the experimental probability of each outcome.
Compare with the theoretical probability and draw a conclusion.
Present your findings with a table, a graph and clear words.

methods.title: How to make and read a tally chart
methods.example: Idris will roll his die 60 times. Write a prediction with a reason.
steps: Make one row for each possible outcome.
Add one mark each time an outcome happens.
Group the marks in fives (IIII) so they are easy to count.
Add up each row to find its frequency.
Check the frequencies add up to your total trials.

methods.title: How to draw a fair conclusion
methods.example: While flipping a coin, Farah records: Heads = IIII IIII IIII, Tails = IIII IIII. How many of each, and how many trials?
steps: Answer your question directly.
Use your numbers as evidence.
Compare the result with your prediction.
Admit any limitations, such as too few trials.
Suggest a follow-up investigation.

methods.title: Work out experimental probability
methods.example: Farah flipped a coin 30 times and got 18 heads. What is the experimental probability of heads?
steps: Solution: Use the rule: frequency ÷ total trials = 18 ÷ 30.
So the experimental probability of heads is 18/30, which simplifies to 3/5.
In plain words, heads came up on three out of every five flips.

methods.title: Compare with theoretical probability
methods.example: For Farah's coin above, the theoretical probability of heads is 1/2. Is her coin fair?
steps: Solution: Her experimental probability was 3/5, which is a little more than 1/2.
That is a small difference, and 30 trials is not very many, so we should say: “The coin looks roughly fair, but heads came up a bit more than expected.
I would flip it many more times to be sure.” We do not jump to calling it unfair.

methods.title: The most common total with two dice
methods.example: Hassan rolls two dice and adds them. He wonders which total is most common. What should he expect, and why?
steps: Solution: The totals run from 2 to 12.
There are more ways to make 7 than any other total: 1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2 and 6+1 – six different ways.
No other total has six ways, so 7 should be the most common.
This is a prediction backed by a clear reason.

workedExamples.title: Turn an idea into a good question
workedExamples.prompt: Idris says, “I wonder about my die.” Turn this into a clear, testable probability question.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A vague wonder must become a sharp question. A good version is: “Is my die fair – does each number from 1 to 6 come up about the same number of times?” This is clear, it is about chance, and Idris can test it by rolling the die many times and counting the results.

workedExamples.title: Make a prediction with a reason
workedExamples.prompt: Idris will roll his die 60 times. Write a prediction with a reason.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: “I predict each number will come up about 10 times, because 60 rolls shared fairly among 6 numbers gives 60 ÷ 6 = 10 each.” The reason (60 ÷ 6 = 10) turns the guess into real thinking. Notice the word “about” – a fair die will rarely give exactly 10 of each, but it should land close.

workedExamples.title: Read a tally chart
workedExamples.prompt: While flipping a coin, Farah records: Heads = IIII IIII IIII, Tails = IIII IIII. How many of each, and how many trials?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Each gate of IIII is five. Heads has three gates: 5 + 5 + 5 = 15. Tails has two gates: 5 + 5 = 10. Total trials = 15 + 10 = 25. Always check the two frequencies add up to the number of trials.

workedExamples.title: Work out experimental probability
workedExamples.prompt: Farah flipped a coin 30 times and got 18 heads. What is the experimental probability of heads?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use the rule: frequency ÷ total trials = 18 ÷ 30. So the experimental probability of heads is 18/30, which simplifies to 3/5. In plain words, heads came up on three out of every five flips.

workedExamples.title: Compare with theoretical probability
workedExamples.prompt: For Farah's coin above, the theoretical probability of heads is 1/2. Is her coin fair?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Her experimental probability was 3/5, which is a little more than 1/2. That is a small difference, and 30 trials is not very many, so we should say: “The coin looks roughly fair, but heads came up a bit more than expected. I would flip it many more times to be sure.” We do not jump to calling it unfair.

workedExamples.title: The most common total with two dice
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan rolls two dice and adds them. He wonders which total is most common. What should he expect, and why?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The totals run from 2 to 12. There are more ways to make 7 than any other total: 1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2 and 6+1 – six different ways. No other total has six ways, so 7 should be the most common. This is a prediction backed by a clear reason.

workedExamples.title: Draw a conclusion from a frequency table
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner with four equal colours is spun 40 times: Red 12, Blue 8, Green 14, Yellow 6. Write a fair conclusion.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The theoretical probability of each colour is 1/4, so we would expect about 10 of each in 40 spins. Green (14) came up more and yellow (6) less. A fair conclusion is: “The spinner was a little uneven – green appeared most and yellow least – but with only 40 spins this may just be luck. More spins are needed before I could say it is truly unfair.”

workedExamples.title: Choose how many trials
workedExamples.prompt: Zahra wants reliable results when testing a coin. She asks whether 5 flips is enough. What should you tell her?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Five flips is far too few – luck could easily give 4 heads and 1 tail by chance, which would fool her. Tell Zahra to do at least 30 flips, and 50 or 100 if she can, because the more trials she does, the closer her results will come to the true chance and the more she can trust them.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Write a prediction, with a reason, for spinning a fair four-colour spinner 40 times.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A spinner is spun 40 times: Red 11, Blue 9, Green 12, Yellow 8. Work out the experimental probability of each colour as a fraction.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Explain why recording results with a tally chart is better than trying to remember them.
workedExamples.solution: A tally is recorded the moment each result happens, so nothing is forgotten or miscounted.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan rolls two dice and adds them. List all the ways to make a total of 7, and explain why 7 is the most likely total.
workedExamples.solution: 1) 1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1 – six ways, more than any other total, so 7 is most likely.

activities.title: The Great Coin Test
activities.materials: a coin (or a bottle cap), paper, a pencil.
steps: Write your question: ‘Is this coin fair?’ and your prediction with a reason.
Make a tally chart with two rows: Heads and Tails.
Flip the coin 50 times, making one tally mark each flip.
Count the frequency of each, then work out the experimental probability of heads (heads ÷ 50).
Compare with the theoretical probability of 1/2 and write a fair conclusion.

activities.title: Is My Die Fair? (Investigation)
steps: Write your prediction: about how many of each number in 60 rolls, and why.
Make a frequency table with a row for each number 1 to 6.
Roll the die 60 times, recording every roll with a tally mark.
Check your frequencies add up to 60, then work out each experimental probability.
Decide: does your die look fair, or do you need more rolls to be sure?

activities.title: The Mystery Bead Bag
activities.materials: a bag or cup, and beads, beans or stones in two or three colours.
steps: Put a secret mix of colours in the bag – have someone else choose the amounts.
Draw one out, record its colour with a tally, then put it back and mix well.
Repeat 40 times, keeping your tally neat.
Work out the experimental probability of each colour.
Guess how many of each colour are really in the bag, then look inside to check!

activities.title: Two-Dice Totals Race (Maths Game)
activities.materials: two dice, paper, a pencil, and a partner.
steps: Write the totals 2 to 12 across the top of your paper.
Predict which total will win by coming up most often, and say why.
Roll the two dice, add them, and put a tally under that total.
Do 40 rolls, then see which total won the race.
Was your prediction right? Explain why 7 usually wins.

activities.title: Can You Beat Luck? (Family Task)
activities.materials: a coin, and several family members or friends.
steps: Ask each person to guess heads or tails before you flip.
Flip the coin and record whether their guess was correct or wrong.
Collect at least 50 guesses in a tally chart.
Work out the experimental probability of a correct guess.
Compare it with 1/2 (pure luck). Can people really beat chance, or not?

activities.title: Present Your Project
activities.materials: your results from any activity above, paper, and colours.
steps: Choose your favourite investigation from this unit.
Draw a bar graph with one bar for each outcome, coloured and labelled.
Write a short report: question, prediction, method, data, and conclusion.
Add one real-world link – who in real life uses chance like this?
Present it to your family or teacher and answer their questions.

rules.title: Experimental Probability Rule
rules.text: Experimental probability = frequency ÷ total number of trials. Example: 11 heads out of 20 flips = 11/20. All the experimental probabilities together add up to one whole.

rules.title: Theoretical Probability Rule
rules.text: Fair coin: 1/2 for heads, 1/2 for tails. Fair die: 1/6 for each of the six numbers. Fair four-colour spinner: 1/4 for each colour.

rules.title: More Trials Rule
rules.text: The more trials you do, the more you can trust your results. Do at least 30 trials; 50 or 100 is even better. A small difference from what you expect is usually just luck.

terms: How likely something is to happen
One single try of the experiment
A possible result of a trial
Marks that count / how many times it happened
What should happen in a fair test
A guess before / what the data shows after
commonMistakes: Calling an object unfair too soon
Experimental = what happened; theoretical = what should

realProblems.prompt: At Kismaayo market, Sumaya flips a bottle cap 60 times to choose who sells first. It lands top-up 33 times. What is the experimental probability of top-up? Is the cap close to fair?
realProblems.answer: 1) 33/60 = 11/20, close to a half, so nearly fair.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) 33/60 = 11/20, close to a half, so nearly fair.

realProblems.prompt: Liban draws a bead from a well-mixed bag 50 times, replacing it each time, and gets green 20 times. What is the experimental probability of green, and how could he make his result more reliable?
realProblems.answer: 20/50 = 2/5; he could make it more reliable by doing many more draws.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 20/50 = 2/5; he could make it more reliable by doing many more draws.

realProblems.prompt: A bus company in Nairobi records that buses were late on 15 out of 60 mornings. What is the experimental probability that a bus is late? Would you trust this more or less than data from only 6 mornings? Why?
realProblems.answer: 15/60 = 1/4; trust it more than 6 mornings, because more trials give more reliable results.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 15/60 = 1/4; trust it more than 6 mornings, because more trials give more reliable results.

realProblems.prompt: Noor asks 20 friends to each guess a coin flip 5 times (100 guesses). They get 52 correct. Is this clearly better than pure luck? Explain your reasoning.
realProblems.answer: 52/100 is barely above the 50 expected from pure luck, so it is not clearly better – more guesses are needed.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 52/100 is barely above the 50 expected from pure luck, so it is not clearly better – more guesses are needed.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Aisha wants to know which of three games children choose most. Describe how she could collect and organise her data, and what graph she could draw.
realProblems.answer: Use a tally chart with a row for each game, count the frequencies, then draw a bar graph with one bar per game.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Use a tally chart with a row for each game, count the frequencies, then draw a bar graph with one bar per game.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan rolls two dice and adds them. List all the ways to make a total of 7, and explain why 7 is the most likely total.
realProblems.answer: 1) 1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1 – six ways, more than any other total, so 7 is most likely.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) 1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1 – six ways, more than any other total, so 7 is most likely.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Hassan rolls two dice and adds them. List all the ways to make a total of 7, and explain why 7 is the most likely total.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) 1+6, 2+5, 3+4, 4+3, 5+2, 6+1 – six ways, more than any other total, so 7 is most likely.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A coin is flipped 100 times and lands heads 58 times. a) What is the experimental probability of heads? b) Is the coin clearly unfair, or could this be luck? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: a) 58/100 = 29/50, b) it is only a little above 1/2, so it could well be luck; more flips are needed before calling it unfair.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A fair die is rolled 60 times. About how many of each number would you expect? Show your working, and explain why you rarely get exactly that many.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: About 10 of each, because 60 ÷ 6 = 10; you rarely get exactly 10 because luck makes each set of trials a little different.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Zahra did only 6 trials and says her spinner is broken. What is wrong with her investigation, and how should she fix it?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Six trials is far too few for chance to settle; she should do at least 30 spins before deciding.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Working Out Experimental Probability.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Once your data is collected and counted, you can work out the experimental probability of each outcome. This is the chance of an outcome based on what actually happened in your experiment. The rule is simple and you will use it many times: Experimental probability = frequency ÷ total number of trials.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Comparing With Theoretical Probability and Drawing Conclusions.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: So far you have found the experimental probability – what actually happened. There is a second kind of chance called the theoretical probability – what should happen if everything is perfectly fair. For a fair coin, the theoretical probability of heads is exactly one half, because there are two equal sides. For a fair die, each number has a theoretical probability of one out of six, because there are six equal faces. For a fair four-colour spinner, each colour has a theoretical probability of one out of four. The…

questions.explanation: 14 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

selfAssessment: I can plan a simple probability investigation by choosing a clear question.
I can make a sensible prediction (a hypothesis) before you begin.
I can decide on your materials and how many trials to carry out.
I can collect data neatly using tally charts and frequency tables.
I can work out the experimental probability of an outcome (how often it happened out of all your trials).
I can compare your results with the theoretical probability (what should happen in a fair test).
I can draw a fair conclusion that answers your question using your data as evidence.
I can present your findings clearly using tables, simple graphs and words.
